species.notes: Must eat fully ripe (after frost); astringent when unripe
Similar to blueberry but with larger seeds; acidic soils
Abundant along trails and field edges; July-August
Multiple species in CT; all edible; lowbush and highbush most common
Also called shadbush; all species edible; sweet mild fruit ripens June
Ripens before blackberries; dark purple-black fruit
Berries must be cooked; raw berries/leaves/stems are toxic
Invasive from Asia; distinctive red hairy canes; tart-sweet fruit
Coastal species; tart fruit excellent for jam
Largest native fruit; tropical custard flavor; southern CT
Highly invasive; abundant small red speckled berries; tart-sweet
Invasive; rose hips high in vitamin C; use for tea/jam
Berries dried as allspice substitute; twigs for tea
Must cook or dry to neutralize stinging hairs; very nutritious
Mild flavor; abundant in lawns and gardens
Wild leek; harvest sustainably (take one leaf and leave bulb)
Invasive; leaves garlicky; harvest freely to help control
Entire plant edible; leaves best in early spring before flowering
Flowers and young leaves edible; mild flavor
Small tubers taste like chestnut; do not overharvest
Must cook thoroughly; only harvest ostrich fern fiddleheads
File powder from leaves (gumbo); root bark for tea
Common garden weed; very nutritious; spinach substitute
Use fresh or dried for teas; found near water
Succulent weed; high in omega-3 fatty acids
Young leaves edible raw; older leaves better cooked
All species edible; lemony flavor; eat in moderation (oxalic acid)
All true morels edible; must cook thoroughly; avoid false morels (Gyromitra)
Grows on dead/dying hardwoods; check for angel wings look-alike
Also called pheasant back; best when young and tender
Grows on dead/dying hardwoods; check for angel wings look-alike
Also called porcini; found under conifers and hardwoods
All true chanterelles edible; golden and cinnabar most common in CT
Choice edible; hard to spot on forest floor
Edible only when interior is pure white; slice to check
Bright orange/yellow bracket fungus on hardwoods; cook thoroughly
Must cook well; deadly Galerina marginata is a look-alike
White shaggy mass on hardwood trees; no dangerous look-alikes
Also called maitake; grows at base of oaks
Edible only when young/white; dissolves into ink quickly
Edible when young and white inside; grows on wood
Grows on dead/dying hardwoods; check for angel wings look-alike
Young shoots and tendrils edible; thorny vine
Buds and flowers edible; some people sensitive; confirm ID carefully
Highly invasive; young shoots taste like rhubarb; harvest freely
Taproot edible first year; peeled stalks also edible
ONLY young spring shoots under 8 inches; must boil twice; berries/roots toxic
Also called sunchoke; nutty tuber; can cause gas
